basis.reasoning: The most directly relevant content describes concrete retry and fallback mechanisms in production-oriented agent systems. A specific production-focused example shows a maximum of three retries with exponential backoff and a requirement to notify a human after exhausting retries, illustrating a cap-and-escalation pattern that prevents infinite loops while preserving recovery attempts. Additional excerpts discuss exponential backoff with jitter, configurable retry counts, rate-limit-aware throttling, and explicit fallback configurations where the primary LLM call can be transparently rerouted to a secondary model when failures occur. Other sources describe health checks and circuit breakers that trigger fallbacks, as well as routing-layer decisions to select fallback providers. Together, these pieces support the idea that modern agentic systems rely on adaptive retries (with backoff and caps), rate-aware throttling, and explicit, automated fallbacks to maintain availability and resilience under transient or persistent failures. The Cursor 2.0 material provides a concrete, widely cited implementation pattern (3 retries with exponential backoff and human notification) that exemplifies how these mechanisms are instantiated in practice. The CrewAI discussions illustrate how to configure a dedicated fallback model and to route around outages, reinforcing the notion that fallbacks are an integral part of production routing decisions. The broader durability-oriented literature contextualizes these as parts of a durable execution stack (temporal, DBOS, etc.), which explains why reliable retry and fallback strategies are essential in long-running, multi-model agent systems.

excerpts: Fenic implements exponential backoff internally when hitting rate limits. The framework automatically throttles requests and resumes when capacity becomes available.
plement tiered models** . Route simple tasks to high-throughput models and advanced tasks to more capable but rate-limited models.
se model profiles** to separate fast inference from deep reasoning workloads
ontrol concurrency** at both the UDF level and server level to prevent rate limit breaches
Large-scale LLM inference operations fail when they hit provider limits. Building production systems requires precise control over token consumption and request throughput.

When processing thousands of documents through LLMs, two bottlenecks emerge:
**Token limits** constrain how much text can be processed in a single request and how many tokens the model generates in its response.
**Rate limits** restrict the number of requests and tokens processed per minute across your entire application.
Both constraints force you to choose between reliability and throughput. Push too hard and requests fail. Throttle too conservatively and pipeline latency explodes.
Rate limit violations and token limit errors require explicit handling.
Failed requests after all retries return null rather than failing the entire batch, maintaining pipeline resilience.
Automatic Retry Logic
citations.title: How to Handle Token Limits and Rate Limits in Large-Scale LLM Inference

excerpts:
On October 29, 2025, Cursor launched version 2.0 with a completely redesigned multi-agent interface and introduced Composer, their first proprietary AI coding model.
The company describes Composer as “a frontier model that is 4x faster than similarly intelligent models,” completing most coding tasks in under 30 seconds.

Composer is a mixture-of-experts language model specialized for software engineering through reinforcement learning in diverse development environments. Here’s what that actually means: the model was trained on real software engineering tasks rather than static datasets, operating inside full codebases using production tools including file editing, semantic search, and terminal commands.
It doesn’t just know syntax. It understands architecture.
citations.title: Meet Cursor 2.0: The AI IDE That Understands Your Entire Codebase | by The Latency Gambler | Medium

excerpts: **Durability:** All workflow state is persisted (“durable virtual memory” ( [[27]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=Using%20code%2C%20it%20aims%20to,whole%20range%20of%20possible%20failures "Highlights: Using code, it aims to,whole range of possible failures") )). Agents can wait hours or days (e.g. for user input) without losing context. This addresses the *long-running workflow disruption* problem.

| **Failure Pattern** | **Description & Example** | **Mitigation / Solution** |
| **Error Propagation** | *Failures cascade through agents.* Without checkpoints, an upstream error corrupts downstream logic. COCO notes lack of correction leads to compounding failures ( [[20]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.13815#:~:text=Large,COCO%20employs%20three "Highlights: Large,COCO employs three") ). | Implement monitoring and retries outside main path. Use stateful rollback: Temporal’s event-sourcing allows replays and compensations rather than naive restarts. Patterns like the Saga (with durable state) can safely compensate partial failures. |

**Fault Tolerance:** Temporal’s workﬂows are **fault-oblivious** ( [[4]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=The%20core%20abstraction%20in%20Temporal,process%20and%20Temporal%20service%20failures "Highlights: The core abstraction in Temporal,process and Temporal service failures") ). If any part of the system crashes, on recover the workflow “picks up right back where [it] started or left off” ( [[28]](https://temporal.io/resources/case-studies/netflix-increases-developer-productivity#:~:text=What%20I%20love%20about%20Temporal,they%20started%20or%20left%20off "Highlights: What I love about Temporal,they started or left off") ). This property is often impossible with stateless orchestration – it means developers can rely on the system to restore agent context after outages.

| **Approach** | **Key Tools/Examples** | **State Handling** | **Pros** | **Cons** |
| **Stateful Workflow Engine** | Temporal, Airflow, Azure Durable, Netflix Conductor | **Persistent, fault-tolerant.** Workflow engine maintains all state. Example: Temporal stores workflow state in database and hides it from developer ( [[4]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=The%20core%20abstraction%20in%20Temporal,process%20and%20Temporal%20service%20failures "Highlights: The core abstraction in Temporal,process and Temporal service failures") ). | Deterministic execution, built-in retries, full observability, easy debugging ( [[21]](https://akka.io/blog/agentic-ai-frameworks#:~:text=A%20centralized%20orchestration%20tool%20is,easier%20debugging%2C%20and%20state%20management "Highlights: A centralized orchestration tool is,easier debugging, and state management") ). | Requires learning engine’s model; central component to manage (though clustering mitigates SPOF). |

Temporal.io exemplifies the kind of solution needed. Its durable, stateful workflow engine directly addresses the key failure modes of agentic systems (fault-tolerance, state synchronization, observability).

n case studies from Snap to enterprise finance, Temporal delivered significantly higher reliability (up to 5-nines uptime ( [[6]](https://www.xgrid.co/resources/modernizing-legacy-infrastructure-unlocks-five-nines-reliability-with-temporal/#:~:text=%2A%2099.999,lost%2C%20even%20during%20system%20failures "Highlights: * 99.999,lost, even during system failures") )) and developer productivity

| **Failure Pattern** | **Description & Example** | **Mitigation / Solution** |
| **Concurrent Data Conflicts** | *Agents write same data concurrently.* Race conditions corrupt state (one write overwrites another) ( [[19]](https://www.getmaxim.ai/articles/multi-agent-system-reliability-failure-patterns-root-causes-and-production-validation-strategies/#:~:text=Conflicting%20State%20Updates%3A%20Multiple%20agents,timing%20rather%20than%20logical%20correctness "Highlights: Conflicting State Updates: Multiple agents,timing rather than logical correctness") ). | Employ transactional workflows or consensus. Orchestrator (like Temporal) sequences activities deterministically. Versioning and idempotent operations prevent overwrites. |

excerpts:
Systems like [DBOS](https://www.dbos.dev/blog/what-is-lightweight-durable-execution) embed durability directly into your application code with minimal overhead. There’s no separate orchestrator to manage, no cluster to configure. Your existing Postgres database becomes your durability layer.
Temporal](https://temporal.io/blog/build-resilient-agentic-ai-with-temporal) represents this heavyweight philosophy. It’s a standalone system designed specifically for managing complex, long-running workflows at scale.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Lightweight Approach: Durability as Code
Imagine adding reliability to your application the same way you add logging — just decorate a function and you’re done.
That’s the promise of **lightweight durable execution** . Systems like [DBOS](https://www.dbos.dev/blog/what-is-lightweight-durable-execution) embed durability directly into your application code with minimal overhead. There’s no separate orchestrator to manage, no cluster to configure. Your existing Postgres database becomes your durability layer.
Here’s what makes it lightweight:
**Minimal setup** — Install a library, connect to Postgres, done
**Automatic checkpointing** — Your function’s state persists as it executes
**Transparent recovery** — Crashes? The function resumes exactly where it stopped
**Developer-friendly** — Feels like writing normal code with annotations
DBOS implements this using Postgres transactions. When a workflow step executes, it commits both the business logic result and the checkpoint in a single atomic transaction. If the process crashes mid-step, Postgres rolls back the entire transaction — guaranteeing exactly-once execution for database operations.
This approach shines for simpler workflows: microservices that need exactly-once guarantees, AI tools that make a few API calls, or embedded agents with straightforward logic.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Heavyweight Approach: Orchestration at Scale
Now imagine you’re coordinating dozens of AI agents, each with complex branching logic, running for days, with sophisticated retry policies and human approval gates.
You need a different tool: a dedicated **orchestration engine** .
[Temporal](https://temporal.io/blog/build-resilient-agentic-ai-with-temporal) represents this heavyweight philosophy. It’s a standalone system designed specifically for managing complex, long-running workflows at scale.
What you get:
**Battle-tested reliability** — Used by companies running millions of workflows
**Rich workflow primitives** — Signals, queries, child workflows, activity retries
**Built-in observability** — Track every state transition in a polished UI
**Horizontal scalability** — Coordinate thousands of concurrent agents
**Strong consistency** — Guaranteed execution semantics through event sourcing
Temporal achieves durability through a different mechanism: every workflow operation gets logged as an event. When a workflow resumes after a failure, Temporal “replays” these events to reconstruct the exact state. This event-sourced architecture enables features like workflow versioning and time-travel debugging.
The tradeoff? More infrastructure to manage. You need to run a Temporal Server cluster (or use Temporal Cloud) separate from your application. But when you need it, nothing else comes close.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > Centralized Orchestration: The Command Center
In a centralized model, one system acts as the “brain” — tracking all agent state, coordinating execution, and ensuring consistency.
[Temporal’s architecture](https://temporal.io/blog/a-mental-model-for-agentic-ai-applications) exemplifies this: a central Temporal Server manages workflow histories while worker nodes execute actual tasks. It’s like air traffic control for your AI agents.
**The wins:**
Single source of truth for what’s happening
Easy to debug (“show me the workflow history”)
Strong consistency guarantees
Unified monitoring and alerting
**The challenges:**
Orchestrator can become a bottleneck at extreme scale
More infrastructure to operate
Potential single point of coordination (though designed for high availability)
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > Decentralized Execution: Autonomous Agents
What if each agent could be self-sufficient, coordinating through shared state or events rather than a central controller?
That’s the decentralized model. [DBOS explores this](https://www.dbos.dev/blog/scaleable-decentralized-workflows) by enabling decentralized durability — agents coordinate through a shared database rather than an orchestrator, with checkpoints living close to execution.
**The wins:**
Scales horizontally without central bottleneck
Better fault isolation (one agent’s crash doesn’t impact others)
More flexible for geographically distributed systems
Lower coordination overhead
**The challenges:**
Harder to maintain global view of system state
Requires careful consistency management
Debugging becomes distributed tracing problem
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > 1. Durable State is Non-Negotiable
Your agent’s progress must survive crashes. Period. This means:
Checkpointing after every significant operation
Persisting intermediate results, not just final output
Using transactions where possible to ensure consistency
Temporal does this through event sourcing (every workflow operation is logged and can be replayed). DBOS does it through Postgres transactions (steps and checkpoints commit atomically). Choose your mechanism, but don’t skip this.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > 6. Versioning and Evolution
Your agent’s logic will change. New version gets deployed while old workflows are still running. You need:
Version-aware execution (old workflows complete on old code)
Migration paths (how do in-flight workflows upgrade?)
Backward compatibility guarantees
As [Temporal’s team emphasizes](https://temporal.io/blog/building-an-agentic-system-thats-actually-production-ready) , production-ready means surviving not just crashes, but deployments and code evolution. Temporal provides explicit versioning mechanisms, while DBOS requires careful schema and code management.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Broader Ecosystem: Beyond Temporal and DBOS
While we’ve focused on Temporal and DBOS as exemplars, the durable execution landscape is rapidly evolving:
**Restate** — A newer event-driven durable execution platform with innovative state management approaches
**AWS Step Functions** — Managed workflows for AWS-native applications (though with vendor lock-in and less flexibility)
**Argo Workflows** — Kubernetes-native orchestration primarily for data and ML pipelines
**Dagster** — Data orchestration platform with durability primitives increasingly used for AI pipelines
**Airflow** — Traditional data workflow orchestrator, though designed for batch operations rather than real-time agentic systems
The trend is clear: **durable execution is becoming foundational infrastructure** for any stateful, autonomous system. Today, that’s primarily AI agents, but tomorrow it could be any long-running, reliable computation.
citations.title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems | by Sai Kumar Yava | Towards AI

Issue #5: Cost & Latency (Slow and Expensive) > Real-world example #3: The retry death spiral
Your system fails occasionally (timeout, rate limit). So you add retry logic: "Try 3 times before giving up."
Normally fine, but when the LLM provider has an outage:
Every request retries 3 times
1,000 concurrent users = 3,000 attempts
Each attempt costs money
You hit rate limits
More retries

basis.reasoning: The most relevant passages explicitly frame Agent Skills as a foundational abstraction for下一 generation agentic AI, describing skills as modular, programmable units that define what an agent should do and how they interoperate with connectors like MCP. They also discuss the open-standardization of agent skills and the practical impact of scaling these libraries, including evidence of routing and selection improvements (e.g., a phase transition in skill selection accuracy as libraries grow, and mechanisms like Tool Search and hierarchical routing that improve effectiveness). These elements directly map to the requested finegrained field value by detailing what Agent Skills are, how they function as a higher-order packaging of procedural knowledge and code, how they connect within an agentic stack, and how routing strategies are used to cope with large skill libraries. The cited materials about taxonomy of skill acquisition methods and the role of MCP further reinforce the concept, while discussions about governance, standards, and real-world adoption provide context for why this paradigm is foundational in 2025–2026. Together, these excerpts substantiate the claim that Agent Skills constitute a foundational abstraction with documented challenges and solutions in large-scale deployments, including hierarchical routing and search techniques that improve skill selection accuracy by a notable margin.

excerpts: Anthropic formalized this concept in October 2025 with the launch of Agent Skills across Claude’s product surface [ [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib2 "Introducing agent skills") ] , followed by its release as an open standard in December 2025 [ [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib4 "Agent skills open standard") ] .

Agent skills have emerged as a foundational abstraction for the next generation of LLM-based agents. By packaging procedural expertise as composable, portable, and dynamically loadable modules, skills resolve the tension between general-purpose models and specialized task requirements. The rapid ecosystem growth—from Anthropic’s October 2025 launch to open standardization in December 2025, with adoption across multiple frontier model providers—confirms that the industry has converged on this abstraction.

Anthropic formalized this concept in October 2025 with the launch of Agent Skills across Claude’s product surface [ [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib2 "Introducing agent skills") ] , followed by its release as an open standard in December 2025 [ [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib4 "Agent skills open standard") ] . Within four months, the anthropics/skills repository accumulated over 62,000 GitHub stars, partner-built skills from Atlassian, Figma, Canva, Stripe, and Notion entered a curated directory, and structurally identical architectures were independently adopted by other frontier model providers.

Table 2 : Taxonomy of skill acquisition methods for LLM agents (2025–2026). Performance metrics are from the original papers on their respective benchmarks.
| Method | Representative Work | Mechanism | Benchmark | Key Result |
| Human-authored | Anthropic Skills [ [35](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib1 "Equipping agents for the real world with agent skills") ] | Manual SKILL.md | Claude products | 62k+ GitHub stars |
| RL with skill library | SAGE [ [29](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib15 "Reinforcement learning for self-improving agent with skill library") ] | GRPO + sequential rollout | AppWorld | +8.9% SGC, 59% tokens |
| Autonomous exploration | SEAgent [ [26](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib20 "SEAgent: self-evolving computer use agent with autonomous learning from experience") ] | Curriculum + world model | OSWorld (5 apps) | 11.3% 34.5% success |
| Structured skill base | CUA-Skill [ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib16 "CUA-Skill: develop skills for computer using agent") ] | Parameterized exec. graphs | WindowsAgentArena | 57.5% SOTA |
| Compositional synthesis | Agentic Proposing [ [12](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib17 "Agentic proposing: enhancing large language model reasoning via compositional skill synthesis") ] | Skill library + GoT agent | AIME 2025 | 91.6% (30B solver) |
| Skill compilation | Li 2026 [ [13](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib18 "When single-agent with skills replace multi-agent systems and when they fail") ] | Multi-agent single-agent | Multiple | Phase transition found |

CUA-Skill [ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib16 "CUA-Skill: develop skills for computer using agent") ] takes a knowledge-engineering approach, encoding human computer-use expertise as parameterized execution graphs and composition graphs . Each skill has typed parameters, preconditions, and composability rules. The CUA-Skill Agent supports dynamic skill retrieval, argument instantiation, and memory-aware failure recovery. On WindowsAgentArena, this achieves a state-of-the-art 57.5% success rate, substantially outperforming approaches that lack structured skill representations.

The rapid adoption of agent skills has introduced a significant and novel attack surface. Unlike traditional software packages, skills combine natural-language instructions with executable code in a format that agents trust implicitly. Three concurrent studies, all appearing between October 2025 and February 2026, provide the first empirical characterization of this threat landscape.

The path forward requires simultaneous advances in multiple directions: more principled skill learning algorithms that produce inspectable artifacts, robust permission models that maintain usability, evaluation frameworks that assess skill quality rather than merely task completion, and governance structures that balance openness with safety.
citations.title: Agent Skills for Large Language Models: Architecture, Acquisition, Security, and the Path Forward

basis.reasoning: The most relevant passages describe a concrete two-level routing approach where the first decision selects a model/provider and the second decision selects the compute/think level for that chosen model. One excerpt explains that the router performs two decisions for every request: which model and how much thinking to do, highlighting the presence of inference-time compute controls and a two-stage decision process. Another excerpt details a two-level routing implementation that respects budget tiers and maps task types to a best model/compute combination for each tier, making the second level explicitly about compute effort rather than model selection alone. Additional passages illustrate that the same task type can be treated differently depending on budget tier, effectively showing dynamic per-task compute level selection within a given model. Other excerpts reinforce this paradigm by discussing cost-performance trade-offs, adaptive thinking controls, and how routing decisions expand beyond choosing a single model to include per-request compute configuration. Collectively, these excerpts demonstrate the two-level routing paradigm as a standard production pattern, enabling per-request optimization of both model choice and reasoning effort within model ecosystems, which directly supports the target finegrained field value.

excerpts: With our `TaskMeta` in hand, we now have enough signal to pick both a model *and* a compute level. This is where the two-level routing from the previous section becomes code. The router respects three budget tiers and maps each task type to the best model/compute combination for that tier.

Look at how the same task type gets different treatment depending on budget tier. A coding task on the “cheap” tier goes to MiniMax M2.5 with low reasoning. That same task on “quality” goes to Claude Opus 4.6 with high adaptive thinking. The router is making *both* decisions: which model and how hard to think.

Research from the CLEAR paper on enterprise agentic evaluation found that optimizing solely for accuracy can yield systems 4.4x to 10.8x more expensive than cost-aware alternatives with comparable performance. Read that again. Comparable performance. Up to 10.8x the cost.

A router doesn’t need to be perfect to pay for itself when these deltas exist. It just needs to avoid consistently spending $25 output tokens on tasks that would be “good enough” at $1 to $3 output tokens.

Think about your last hundred API calls. How many were simple Q&A, summarization, or formatting tasks that got routed to your most expensive model by default? That’s the “overpaying zone” that routing eliminates.

The CLEAR paper’s 4.4x to 10.8x cost multipliers give you a defensible range to compare against before you even collect your own data. If your single-model baseline falls inside that range, routing is already justified.
The only thing getting more expensive is *not* routing.

MiniMax M2.5** is the budget play that doesn’t feel like a compromise. Its published token prices are an order of magnitude below premium frontier pricing while still posting competitive coding and agent-tool benchmarks. This is your throughput le

Here’s what separates a basic router from a production-grade one. In February 2026, “which model” is no longer the only routing decision. Providers now expose inference-time compute controls, which means you need *two* decisions for every request.
wo-Level Routing** : Why choosing the right model is only half the decision, and how inference-time compute controls (reasoning effort, thinking levels) unlock the other hal

| Skill compilation | Li 2026 [ [13](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib18 "When single-agent with skills replace multi-agent systems and when they fail") ] | Multi-agent single-agent | Multiple | Phase transition found |

CUA-Skill [ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib16 "CUA-Skill: develop skills for computer using agent") ] takes a knowledge-engineering approach, encoding human computer-use expertise as parameterized execution graphs and composition graphs . Each skill has typed parameters, preconditions, and composability rules. The CUA-Skill Agent supports dynamic skill retrieval, argument instantiation, and memory-aware failure recovery. On WindowsAgentArena, this achieves a state-of-the-art 57.5% success rate, substantially outperforming approaches that lack structured skill representations.

The rapid adoption of agent skills has introduced a significant and novel attack surface. Unlike traditional software packages, skills combine natural-language instructions with executable code in a format that agents trust implicitly. Three concurrent studies, all appearing between October 2025 and February 2026, provide the first empirical characterization of this threat landscape.

The path forward requires simultaneous advances in multiple directions: more principled skill learning algorithms that produce inspectable artifacts, robust permission models that maintain usability, evaluation frameworks that assess skill quality rather than merely task completion, and governance structures that balance openness with safety.
citations.title: Agent Skills for Large Language Models: Architecture, Acquisition, Security, and the Path Forward

basis.reasoning: Direct discussion of a retry death spiral describes how repeated retries after provider outages can overwhelm a system, trigger rate limits, and incur high costs, all of which undermine security and reliability. The mitigation guidance advocates a multi-layered governance approach with global retry budgets, intelligent exponential backoff with jitter, explicit per-provider concurrency controls, and graceful degradation or escalation to human operators when retries are exhausted. This directly supports the field value by outlining concrete security-conscious controls to prevent cascading failures. Additional excerpts describe practical retry limits and bounded retry behavior in production-ready frameworks, including a capped number of retries and exponential backoff, which are essential for avoiding an unchecked spiral that could be exploited or cause financial loss. The inclusion of fallback strategies (explicit fallback_llm to secondary providers) highlights resilience against provider outages, a critical security/continuity control in agentic systems. Other excerpts discuss durable execution and state persistence (e.g., Temporal, DBOS, LangGraph) that enable replay, rollback, and recovery without compromising integrity or exposing systems to uncontrolled state changes. Together, these excerpts sketch a defense-in-depth approach: govern and throttle retries, implement safe degradation pathways, ensure memory of past interactions with safe replay, and provide reliable fallbacks, all of which reduce the risk of security and reliability breaches due to retry storms or cascading failures. In addition, discussions of token and rate limits handling and tiered model usage illustrate operational controls that prevent overuse of resources during faults, further mitigating security and cost risks in agentic production environments.

excerpts: Automatic Retry Logic
Large-scale LLM inference operations fail when they hit provider limits. Building production systems requires precise control over token consumption and request throughput.

When processing thousands of documents through LLMs, two bottlenecks emerge:
**Token limits** constrain how much text can be processed in a single request and how many tokens the model generates in its response.
**Rate limits** restrict the number of requests and tokens processed per minute across your entire application.
Both constraints force you to choose between reliability and throughput. Push too hard and requests fail. Throttle too conservatively and pipeline latency explodes.
Rate limit violations and token limit errors require explicit handling.
Failed requests after all retries return null rather than failing the entire batch, maintaining pipeline resilience.
Fenic implements exponential backoff internally when hitting rate limits. The framework automatically throttles requests and resumes when capacity becomes available.

If any part of the system crashes, on recover the workflow “picks up right back where [it] started or left off” ( [[28]](https://temporal.io/resources/case-studies/netflix-increases-developer-productivity#:~:text=What%20I%20love%20about%20Temporal,they%20started%20or%20left%20off "Highlights: What I love about Temporal,they started or left off") ). This property is often impossible with stateless orchestration – it means developers can rely on the system to restore agent context after outages.

Example: Temporal stores workflow state in database and hides it from developer ( [[4]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=The%20core%20abstraction%20in%20Temporal,process%20and%20Temporal%20service%20failures "Highlights: The core abstraction in Temporal,process and Temporal service failures") ). | Deterministic execution, built-in retries, full observability, easy debugging ( [[21]](https://akka.io/blog/agentic-ai-frameworks#:~:text=A%20centralized%20orchestration%20tool%20is,easier%20debugging%2C%20and%20state%20management "Highlights: A centralized orchestration tool is,easier debugging, and state management") ). | Requires learning engine’s model; central component to manage (though clustering mitigates SPOF). |

excerpts:
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Heavyweight Approach: Orchestration at Scale
Now imagine you’re coordinating dozens of AI agents, each with complex branching logic, running for days, with sophisticated retry policies and human approval gates.
You need a different tool: a dedicated **orchestration engine** .
[Temporal](https://temporal.io/blog/build-resilient-agentic-ai-with-temporal) represents this heavyweight philosophy. It’s a standalone system designed specifically for managing complex, long-running workflows at scale.
What you get:
**Battle-tested reliability** — Used by companies running millions of workflows
**Rich workflow primitives** — Signals, queries, child workflows, activity retries
**Built-in observability** — Track every state transition in a polished UI
**Horizontal scalability** — Coordinate thousands of concurrent agents
**Strong consistency** — Guaranteed execution semantics through event sourcing
Temporal achieves durability through a different mechanism: every workflow operation gets logged as an event. When a workflow resumes after a failure, Temporal “replays” these events to reconstruct the exact state. This event-sourced architecture enables features like workflow versioning and time-travel debugging.
The tradeoff? More infrastructure to manage. You need to run a Temporal Server cluster (or use Temporal Cloud) separate from your application. But when you need it, nothing else comes close.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > Centralized Orchestration: The Command Center
In a centralized model, one system acts as the “brain” — tracking all agent state, coordinating execution, and ensuring consistency.
[Temporal’s architecture](https://temporal.io/blog/a-mental-model-for-agentic-ai-applications) exemplifies this: a central Temporal Server manages workflow histories while worker nodes execute actual tasks. It’s like air traffic control for your AI agents.
**The wins:**
Single source of truth for what’s happening
Easy to debug (“show me the workflow history”)
Strong consistency guarantees
Unified monitoring and alerting
**The challenges:**
Orchestrator can become a bottleneck at extreme scale
More infrastructure to operate
Potential single point of coordination (though designed for high availability)
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > Decentralized Execution: Autonomous Agents
What if each agent could be self-sufficient, coordinating through shared state or events rather than a central controller?
That’s the decentralized model. [DBOS explores this](https://www.dbos.dev/blog/scaleable-decentralized-workflows) by enabling decentralized durability — agents coordinate through a shared database rather than an orchestrator, with checkpoints living close to execution.
**The wins:**
Scales horizontally without central bottleneck
Better fault isolation (one agent’s crash doesn’t impact others)
More flexible for geographically distributed systems
Lower coordination overhead
**The challenges:**
Harder to maintain global view of system state
Requires careful consistency management
Debugging becomes distributed tracing problem
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > 1. Durable State is Non-Negotiable
Your agent’s progress must survive crashes. Period. This means:
Checkpointing after every significant operation
Persisting intermediate results, not just final output
Using transactions where possible to ensure consistency
Temporal does this through event sourcing (every workflow operation is logged and can be replayed). DBOS does it through Postgres transactions (steps and checkpoints commit atomically). Choose your mechanism, but don’t skip this.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > 6. Versioning and Evolution
Your agent’s logic will change. New version gets deployed while old workflows are still running. You need:
Version-aware execution (old workflows complete on old code)
Migration paths (how do in-flight workflows upgrade?)
Backward compatibility guarantees
As [Temporal’s team emphasizes](https://temporal.io/blog/building-an-agentic-system-thats-actually-production-ready) , production-ready means surviving not just crashes, but deployments and code evolution. Temporal provides explicit versioning mechanisms, while DBOS requires careful schema and code management.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Broader Ecosystem: Beyond Temporal and DBOS
While we’ve focused on Temporal and DBOS as exemplars, the durable execution landscape is rapidly evolving:
**Restate** — A newer event-driven durable execution platform with innovative state management approaches
**AWS Step Functions** — Managed workflows for AWS-native applications (though with vendor lock-in and less flexibility)
**Argo Workflows** — Kubernetes-native orchestration primarily for data and ML pipelines
**Dagster** — Data orchestration platform with durability primitives increasingly used for AI pipelines
**Airflow** — Traditional data workflow orchestrator, though designed for batch operations rather than real-time agentic systems
The trend is clear: **durable execution is becoming foundational infrastructure** for any stateful, autonomous system. Today, that’s primarily AI agents, but tomorrow it could be any long-running, reliable computation.
citations.title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems | by Sai Kumar Yava | Towards AI

excerpts: evel 1** : Which model family/provider handles the task? **Level 2** : Within that model, what reasoning/compute level is warra

With our `TaskMeta` in hand, we now have enough signal to pick both a model *and* a compute level. This is where the two-level routing from the previous section becomes code. The router respects three budget tiers and maps each task type to the best model/compute combination for that tier.

Look at how the same task type gets different treatment depending on budget tier. A coding task on the “cheap” tier goes to MiniMax M2.5 with low reasoning. That same task on “quality” goes to Claude Opus 4.6 with high adaptive thinking. The router is making *both* decisions: which model and how hard to think.

excerpts:
On October 29, 2025, Cursor launched version 2.0 with a completely redesigned multi-agent interface and introduced Composer, their first proprietary AI coding model.
The company describes Composer as “a frontier model that is 4x faster than similarly intelligent models,” completing most coding tasks in under 30 seconds.

Composer is a mixture-of-experts language model specialized for software engineering through reinforcement learning in diverse development environments. Here’s what that actually means: the model was trained on real software engineering tasks rather than static datasets, operating inside full codebases using production tools including file editing, semantic search, and terminal commands.
It doesn’t just know syntax. It understands architecture.

excerpts: Enter Devin AI, an AI software engineer developed by Cognition Labs, designed to execute tasks across the entire software development lifecycle.

Unlike most generative AI tools that react passively to prompts, Devin represents a newer class of agentic AI: a proactive contributor that feels like collaborating with a highly capable junior developer who works tirelessly.
After recently experimenting with Devin, it is clear that AI software engineers can transform development workflows in multiple ways.
Devin’s role as a proactive contributor means it has the potential to be a productivity multiplier, but only when applied appropriately:

sting** : Devin can generate and maintain “happy path” tests automatically, reducing some manual effort, but it struggles with complex edge cases and nuanced domain logic, which still demand careful human oversight.

Debugging** : Devin can run code, inspect logs, trace issues, apply corrections, and retry, turning routine debugging into a procedural exercise. However, it fails on more complex bugs and bugs that are difficult to reproduce, which still require human intervention to resolv

ask Execution** : Devin can complete small, well-defined tasks quickly, but its narrow focus on finishing tasks rather than considering long-term architecture often creates technical debt that must be actively managed and refactored

Section Title: Introducing SWE-grep and SWE-grep-mini: RL for Multi-Turn, Fast Context Retrieval
Content:
by Ben Pan, Carlo Baronio, Albert Tam, Pietro Marsella, Mokshit Jain, Daniel Chiu, Swyx, Silas Alberti
In this article:
**TL;DR: We trained SWE-grep and SWE-grep-mini, fast agentic models specialized in highly parallel context retrieval. They match the retrieval capabilities of frontier coding models, while taking an order of magnitude less time. Available now in Windsurf’s new Fast Context subagent!**
In Windsurf and Devin, we observed that our agent trajectories were often spending >60% of their first turn just retrieving context.
This **speed-intelligence tradeoff** seemed inescapable — until we trained **SWE-grep** and **SWE-grep-mini:** models which match the retrieval capabilities of frontier coding models, while taking an order of magnitude less time. These models now power **Fast Context** , a subagent that helps you stay in flow.
We thus trained the `SWE-grep` models to natively issue up to 8 parallel tool calls per turn in a maximum of 4 turns (3 turns of exploration and 1 turn for the answer).
citations.title: Cognition | Introducing SWE-grep and SWE-grep-mini: RL for Multi-Turn, Fast Context Retrieval

excerpts: LangGraph has a built-in persistence layer, implemented through checkpointers. When you compile a graph with a checkpointer, the checkpointer saves a `checkpoint` of the graph state at every super-step. Those checkpoints are saved to a `thread` , which can be accessed after graph execution. Because `threads` allow access to graph’s state after execution, several powerful capabilities including human-in-the-loop, memory, time travel, and fault-tolerance are all possible.
The state of a thread at a particular point in time is called a checkpoint. Checkpoint is a snapshot of the graph state saved at each super-step
Checkpoints are persisted and can be used to restore the state of a thread at a later time.

It’s also possible to play-back a prior graph execution. If we `invoke` a graph with a `thread_id` and a `checkpoint_id` , then we will *re-play* the previously executed steps *before* a checkpoint that corresponds to the `checkpoint_id` , and only execute the steps *after* the checkpoint.

Importantly, LangGraph knows whether a particular step has been executed previously. If it has, LangGraph simply *re-plays* that particular step in the graph and does not re-execute the step, but only for the steps *before* the provided `checkpoint_id` . All of the steps *after* `checkpoint_id` will be executed (i.e., a new fork), even if they have been executed previously.

Checkpointers can optionally encrypt all persisted state. To enable this, pass an instance of [`EncryptedSerializer`](https://reference.langchain.com/python/langgraph/checkpoints/.checkpoint.serde.encrypted.EncryptedSerializer) to the `serde` argument of any [`BaseCheckpointSaver`](https://reference.langchain.com/python/langgraph/checkpoints/.checkpoint.base.BaseCheckpointSaver) implementation.

First, checkpointers facilitate human-in-the-loop workflows by allowing humans to inspect, interrupt, and approve graph steps. Checkpointers are needed for these workflows as the human has to be able to view the state of a graph at any point in time, and the graph has to be to resume execution after the human has made any updates to the state.

Second, checkpointers allow for “memory” between interactions. In the case of repeated human interactions (like conversations) any follow up messages can be sent to that thread, which will retain its memory of previous ones.

Third, checkpointers allow for “time travel” , allowing users to replay prior graph executions to review and / or debug specific graph steps. In addition, checkpointers make it possible to fork the graph state at arbitrary checkpoints to explore alternative trajectories.

Lastly, checkpointing also provides fault-tolerance and error recovery: if one or more nodes fail at a given superstep, you can restart your graph from the last successful step. Additionally, when a graph node fails mid-execution at a given superstep, LangGraph stores pending checkpoint writes from any other nodes that completed successfully at that superstep, so that whenever we resume graph execution from that superstep we don’t re-run the successful nodes.

Additionally, when a graph node fails mid-execution at a given superstep, LangGraph stores pending checkpoint writes from any other nodes that completed successfully at that superstep, so that whenever we resume graph execution from that superstep we don’t re-run the successful nodes.
LangGraph

excerpts: This library defines the base interface for [LangGraph.js](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraphjs) checkpointers. Checkpointers provide persistence layer for LangGraph. They allow you to interact with and manage the graph's state. When you use a graph with a checkpointer, the checkpointer saves a *checkpoint* of the graph state at every superstep, enabling several powerful capabilities like human-in-the-loop, "memory" between interactions and more.
Checkpoint is a snapshot of the graph state at a given point in time. Checkpoint tuple refers to an object containing checkpoint and the associated config, metadata and pending writes.

When a graph node fails mid-execution at a given superstep, LangGraph stores pending checkpoint writes from any other nodes that completed successfully at that superstep, so that whenever we resume graph execution from that superstep we don't re-run the successful nodes.

citations.title: Changelog - CrewAI

excerpts: When `memory=True` , the crew creates a default `Memory()` and passes the crew’s `embedder` configuration through automatically. All agents in the crew share the crew’s memory unless an agent has its own. After each task, the crew automatically extracts discrete facts from the task output and stores them. Before each task, the agent recalls relevant context from memory and injects it into the task prompt.

Read Barrier
Every `recall()` call automatically calls `drain_writes()` before searching, ensuring the query always sees the latest persisted records. This is transparent — you never need to think about it.

Crew Shutdown
When a crew finishes, `kickoff()` drains all pending memory saves in its `finally` block, so no saves are lost even if the crew completes while background saves are in flight.

ckground save errors in logs?**
Memory saves run in a background thread. Errors are emitted as `MemorySaveFailedEvent` but don’t crash the agent.

LanceDB operations are serialized with a shared lock and retried automatically on conflict. This handles multiple `Memory` instances pointing at the same database (e.g. agent memory + crew memory). No action needed.

emory not persisting?**
Ensure the storage path is writable (default `./.crewai/memory` ). Pass `storage="./your_path"` to use a different directory, or set the `CREWAI_STORAGE_DIR` environment variable.
When using a crew, confirm `memory=True` or `memory=Memory(...)` is set

They detail how skills are packed, shared, and governed, including open standards and ecosystem adoption, which aligns with the envisioned robust ecosystems for discovering, composing, and securely executing skills across different agent frameworks and platforms.

- References to Agent2Agent integration and cross-provider interoperability point to standardized signals and schemas for interoperability, which supports the idea of unified formats for partial/resumable outputs and cross-provider fallbacks.
- Discussions of governance, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and permission models (as part of agent skills governance and the broader agentic stack) map to formalizing governance frameworks for long-running autonomous tasks, including auditable agent behavior.
- Durability and reliability perspectives (durable execution, memory, and stateful orchestration) underpin the practical need for robust, auditable state management as routing and governance mature, ensuring recoverability and traceability across platforms.
- The combination of these themes—standardized skills, governance, cross-platform portability, and intelligent routing meta-models—collectively supports the projected future trajectory where routing is adaptive, interoperable, and governed by formalized standards and signals.

excerpts: Anthropic formalized this concept in October 2025 with the launch of Agent Skills across Claude’s product surface [ [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib2 "Introducing agent skills") ] , followed by its release as an open standard in December 2025 [ [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib4 "Agent skills open standard") ] . Within four months, the anthropics/skills repository accumulated over 62,000 GitHub stars, partner-built skills from Atlassian, Figma, Canva, Stripe, and Notion entered a curated directory, and structurally identical architectures were independently adopted by other frontier model providers.

The path forward requires simultaneous advances in multiple directions: more principled skill learning algorithms that produce inspectable artifacts, robust permission models that maintain usability, evaluation frameworks that assess skill quality rather than merely task completion, and governance structures that balance openness with safety.

The rapid adoption of agent skills has introduced a significant and novel attack surface. Unlike traditional software packages, skills combine natural-language instructions with executable code in a format that agents trust implicitly. Three concurrent studies, all appearing between October 2025 and February 2026, provide the first empirical characterization of this threat landscape.

CUA-Skill [ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib16 "CUA-Skill: develop skills for computer using agent") ] takes a knowledge-engineering approach, encoding human computer-use expertise as parameterized execution graphs and composition graphs . Each skill has typed parameters, preconditions, and composability rules. The CUA-Skill Agent supports dynamic skill retrieval, argument instantiation, and memory-aware failure recovery. On WindowsAgentArena, this achieves a state-of-the-art 57.5% success rate, substantially outperforming approaches that lack structured skill representations.

Table 2 : Taxonomy of skill acquisition methods for LLM agents (2025–2026). Performance metrics are from the original papers on their respective benchmarks.
| Method | Representative Work | Mechanism | Benchmark | Key Result |
| Human-authored | Anthropic Skills [ [35](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib1 "Equipping agents for the real world with agent skills") ] | Manual SKILL.md | Claude products | 62k+ GitHub stars |
| RL with skill library | SAGE [ [29](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib15 "Reinforcement learning for self-improving agent with skill library") ] | GRPO + sequential rollout | AppWorld | +8.9% SGC, 59% tokens |
| Autonomous exploration | SEAgent [ [26](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib20 "SEAgent: self-evolving computer use agent with autonomous learning from experience") ] | Curriculum + world model | OSWorld (5 apps) | 11.3% 34.5% success |
| Structured skill base | CUA-Skill [ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib16 "CUA-Skill: develop skills for computer using agent") ] | Parameterized exec. graphs | WindowsAgentArena | 57.5% SOTA |
| Compositional synthesis | Agentic Proposing [ [12](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib17 "Agentic proposing: enhancing large language model reasoning via compositional skill synthesis") ] | Skill library + GoT agent | AIME 2025 | 91.6% (30B solver) |
| Skill compilation | Li 2026 [ [13](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib18 "When single-agent with skills replace multi-agent systems and when they fail") ] | Multi-agent single-agent | Multiple | Phase transition found |

Anthropic formalized this concept in October 2025 with the launch of Agent Skills across Claude’s product surface [ [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib2 "Introducing agent skills") ] , followed by its release as an open standard in December 2025 [ [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib4 "Agent skills open standard") ] .
Together, skills and MCP define an emerging agentic stack in which skills supply the “what to do” and MCP supplies the “how to connect.”

Skill engineering (2025–present) introduces a higher-order abstraction. A skill is a bundle that can include instructions, workflow guidance, executable scripts, reference documentation, and metadata, all organized to be dynamically loaded when relevant.

Li [ [13](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib18 "When single-agent with skills replace multi-agent systems and when they fail") ] identified a phase transition in skill selection accuracy as library size grows. As enterprise skill libraries scale to hundreds or thousands of skills, the routing problem—determining which skill(s) to activate for a given query—becomes combinatorially challenging. Advanced Tool Use features [ [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.12430v3.bib9 "Introducing advanced tool use on the claude developer platform") ] address this partially through Tool Search, but the fundamental scaling problem persists.
citations.title: Agent Skills for Large Language Models: Architecture, Acquisition, Security, and the Path Forward

excerpts:
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Broader Ecosystem: Beyond Temporal and DBOS
While we’ve focused on Temporal and DBOS as exemplars, the durable execution landscape is rapidly evolving:
**Restate** — A newer event-driven durable execution platform with innovative state management approaches
**AWS Step Functions** — Managed workflows for AWS-native applications (though with vendor lock-in and less flexibility)
**Argo Workflows** — Kubernetes-native orchestration primarily for data and ML pipelines
**Dagster** — Data orchestration platform with durability primitives increasingly used for AI pipelines
**Airflow** — Traditional data workflow orchestrator, though designed for batch operations rather than real-time agentic systems
The trend is clear: **durable execution is becoming foundational infrastructure** for any stateful, autonomous system. Today, that’s primarily AI agents, but tomorrow it could be any long-running, reliable computation.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Heavyweight Approach: Orchestration at Scale
Now imagine you’re coordinating dozens of AI agents, each with complex branching logic, running for days, with sophisticated retry policies and human approval gates.
You need a different tool: a dedicated **orchestration engine** .
[Temporal](https://temporal.io/blog/build-resilient-agentic-ai-with-temporal) represents this heavyweight philosophy. It’s a standalone system designed specifically for managing complex, long-running workflows at scale.
What you get:
**Battle-tested reliability** — Used by companies running millions of workflows
**Rich workflow primitives** — Signals, queries, child workflows, activity retries
**Built-in observability** — Track every state transition in a polished UI
**Horizontal scalability** — Coordinate thousands of concurrent agents
**Strong consistency** — Guaranteed execution semantics through event sourcing
Temporal achieves durability through a different mechanism: every workflow operation gets logged as an event. When a workflow resumes after a failure, Temporal “replays” these events to reconstruct the exact state. This event-sourced architecture enables features like workflow versioning and time-travel debugging.
The tradeoff? More infrastructure to manage. You need to run a Temporal Server cluster (or use Temporal Cloud) separate from your application. But when you need it, nothing else comes close.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > Centralized Orchestration: The Command Center
In a centralized model, one system acts as the “brain” — tracking all agent state, coordinating execution, and ensuring consistency.
[Temporal’s architecture](https://temporal.io/blog/a-mental-model-for-agentic-ai-applications) exemplifies this: a central Temporal Server manages workflow histories while worker nodes execute actual tasks. It’s like air traffic control for your AI agents.
**The wins:**
Single source of truth for what’s happening
Easy to debug (“show me the workflow history”)
Strong consistency guarantees
Unified monitoring and alerting
**The challenges:**
Orchestrator can become a bottleneck at extreme scale
More infrastructure to operate
Potential single point of coordination (though designed for high availability)
citations.title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems | by Sai Kumar Yava | Towards AI

excerpts: Temporal.io (henceforth *Temporal* ) is one such solution – an open-source, cloud-native **stateful workflow and orchestration engine** .

Temporal transparently handles retries, state persistence, and timeouts, providing *“durable execution”* across failures ( [[4]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=The%20core%20abstraction%20in%20Temporal,process%20and%20Temporal%20service%20failures "Highlights: The core abstraction in Temporal,process and Temporal service failures") ).

By centralizing workflow logic, preserving state across process boundaries, and offering built-in reliability (fault tolerance, logging, observability, etc. ), Temporal allows AI-driven workflows to be **scalable, debuggable, and resilient** in production.

| **Long-Running Workflow Disruption** | *Agent pauses or system restart mid-flow.* E.g., an agent waits days for human approval, but intermediate state is lost on crash. | Utilize durable state persistence. Workflow engines snapshot context; after downtime, the agent resumes automatically at last known point. (Temporal’s durable virtual memory survives process restarts ( [[4]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=The%20core%20abstraction%20in%20Temporal,process%20and%20Temporal%20service%20failures "Highlights: The core abstraction in Temporal,process and Temporal service failures") ).)
| **Approach** | **Key Tools/Examples** | **State Handling** | **Pros** | **Cons** |
| **Stateful Workflow Engine** | Temporal, Airflow, Azure Durable, Netflix Conductor | **Persistent, fault-tolerant.** Workflow engine maintains all state. Example: Temporal stores workflow state in database and hides it from developer ( [[4]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=The%20core%20abstraction%20in%20Temporal,process%20and%20Temporal%20service%20failures "Highlights: The core abstraction in Temporal,process and Temporal service failures") ). | Deterministic execution, built-in retries, full observability, easy debugging ( [[21]](https://akka.io/blog/agentic-ai-frameworks#:~:text=A%20centralized%20orchestration%20tool%20is,easier%20debugging%2C%20and%20state%20management "Highlights: A centralized orchestration tool is,easier debugging, and state management") ). | Requires learning engine’s model; central component to manage (though clustering mitigates SPOF). |

**Durability:** All workflow state is persisted (“durable virtual memory” ( [[27]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=Using%20code%2C%20it%20aims%20to,whole%20range%20of%20possible%20failures "Highlights: Using code, it aims to,whole range of possible failures") )). Agents can wait hours or days (e.g. for user input) without losing context. This addresses the *long-running workflow disruption* problem.

| **Failure Pattern** | **Description & Example** | **Mitigation / Solution** |
| **Error Propagation** | *Failures cascade through agents.* Without checkpoints, an upstream error corrupts downstream logic. COCO notes lack of correction leads to compounding failures ( [[20]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.13815#:~:text=Large,COCO%20employs%20three "Highlights: Large,COCO employs three") ). | Implement monitoring and retries outside main path. Use stateful rollback: Temporal’s event-sourcing allows replays and compensations rather than naive restarts. Patterns like the Saga (with durable state) can safely compensate partial failures. |

**Fault Tolerance:** Temporal’s workﬂows are **fault-oblivious** ( [[4]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=The%20core%20abstraction%20in%20Temporal,process%20and%20Temporal%20service%20failures "Highlights: The core abstraction in Temporal,process and Temporal service failures") ). If any part of the system crashes, on recover the workflow “picks up right back where [it] started or left off” ( [[28]](https://temporal.io/resources/case-studies/netflix-increases-developer-productivity#:~:text=What%20I%20love%20about%20Temporal,they%20started%20or%20left%20off "Highlights: What I love about Temporal,they started or left off") ). This property is often impossible with stateless orchestration – it means developers can rely on the system to restore agent context after outages.

| **Approach** | **Key Tools/Examples** | **State Handling** | **Pros** | **Cons** |
| **Stateful Workflow Engine** | Temporal, Airflow, Azure Durable, Netflix Conductor | **Persistent, fault-tolerant.** Workflow engine maintains all state. Example: Temporal stores workflow state in database and hides it from developer ( [[4]](https://thenewstack.io/temporal-tackles-microservice-reliability-headaches/#:~:text=The%20core%20abstraction%20in%20Temporal,process%20and%20Temporal%20service%20failures "Highlights: The core abstraction in Temporal,process and Temporal service failures") ). | Deterministic execution, built-in retries, full observability, easy debugging ( [[21]](https://akka.io/blog/agentic-ai-frameworks#:~:text=A%20centralized%20orchestration%20tool%20is,easier%20debugging%2C%20and%20state%20management "Highlights: A centralized orchestration tool is,easier debugging, and state management") ). | Requires learning engine’s model; central component to manage (though clustering mitigates SPOF). |

Temporal.io exemplifies the kind of solution needed. Its durable, stateful workflow engine directly addresses the key failure modes of agentic systems (fault-tolerance, state synchronization, observability).

n case studies from Snap to enterprise finance, Temporal delivered significantly higher reliability (up to 5-nines uptime ( [[6]](https://www.xgrid.co/resources/modernizing-legacy-infrastructure-unlocks-five-nines-reliability-with-temporal/#:~:text=%2A%2099.999,lost%2C%20even%20during%20system%20failures "Highlights: * 99.999,lost, even during system failures") )) and developer productivity

basis.reasoning: The most directly supportive material comes from discussions of cost-aware routing and quantified cost multipliers. The cited material explains that optimizing for cost rather than solely for accuracy can yield substantial savings (4x–10x reductions) while maintaining comparable performance, and it explicitly references the CLEAR paper’s finding that accuracy-focused approaches can be substantially more expensive (4.4x–10.8x) for similar results. This aligns precisely with the finegrained field value, which frames model routing as a major lever for economic viability and scale in production. Supporting context shows that production-minded routing frameworks increasingly separate decisions about which model to use from how hard each model should think, introducing compute-level controls and tiered strategies that optimize cost without sacrificing outcomes. Additional excerpts describe practical production-oriented routing architectures (two-level routing, task classifiers, policies, fallbacks) and emphasize monitoring, adaptability, and cost-aware decision making as core design principles. Together, these excerpts sketch a coherent picture: organizations should employ cost-aware routing as a central, measurable economic strategy, use tiered and adaptive routing to curb compute spend, and consult empirical analyses (like CLEAR) to benchmark potential savings. This combination of explicit cost savings figures, methodological contrast (cost-aware vs accuracy-focused), and concrete production guidance provides strong support for the field value. The logic flows from the assertion that routing strategy strongly impacts cost, through quantified evidence of savings, to practical, production-ready approaches that realize those savings in real systems.

The CLEAR paper’s 4.4x to 10.8x cost multipliers give you a defensible range to compare against before you even collect your own data. If your single-model baseline falls inside that range, routing is already justified.

basis.reasoning: The most relevant information directly demonstrates resilience techniques for production LLM inference. Exponential backoff with automatic retries and explicit handling when retries are exhausted (returning a null or non-blocking result for a failed item) provide concrete strategies for transient errors without stalling entire batches. The practice of returning null after all retries in batch contexts aligns with graceful degradation and partial-result resilience in streaming or batched inference workloads. Checkpointing and persistent memory enable resumption from the last successful state, ensuring long-running tasks and workflows can continue after interruptions and re-use prior inputs or partial outputs. Durable, stateful orchestration platforms illustrate how to maintain a single source of truth for agent state, coordinate retries, and recover from failures without losing context. Latency-aware strategies such as hedged requests show how production systems can mitigate high-latency regions or providers by parallelizing calls and accepting the first valid response, assuming idempotency. Governance angles (retry budgets) and resource-budgeting discussions help constrain aggressive retry behavior and prevent cascading failures, while per-task token and throughput controls guard against rate-limit violations. Together, these excerpts support a multi-layer resilience model: robust transient-error handling with backoff, controlled retries with graceful degradation, checkpointed progression for partial outputs, durable orchestration with centralized or distributed state, latency hedging for SLA adherence, and governance/ budget controls to prevent retry spirals and manage resources.

excerpts: LangGraph has a built-in persistence layer, implemented through checkpointers. When you compile a graph with a checkpointer, the checkpointer saves a `checkpoint` of the graph state at every super-step. Those checkpoints are saved to a `thread` , which can be accessed after graph execution. Because `threads` allow access to graph’s state after execution, several powerful capabilities including human-in-the-loop, memory, time travel, and fault-tolerance are all possible.

excerpts:
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Heavyweight Approach: Orchestration at Scale
Now imagine you’re coordinating dozens of AI agents, each with complex branching logic, running for days, with sophisticated retry policies and human approval gates.
You need a different tool: a dedicated **orchestration engine** .
[Temporal](https://temporal.io/blog/build-resilient-agentic-ai-with-temporal) represents this heavyweight philosophy. It’s a standalone system designed specifically for managing complex, long-running workflows at scale.
What you get:
**Battle-tested reliability** — Used by companies running millions of workflows
**Rich workflow primitives** — Signals, queries, child workflows, activity retries
**Built-in observability** — Track every state transition in a polished UI
**Horizontal scalability** — Coordinate thousands of concurrent agents
**Strong consistency** — Guaranteed execution semantics through event sourcing
Temporal achieves durability through a different mechanism: every workflow operation gets logged as an event. When a workflow resumes after a failure, Temporal “replays” these events to reconstruct the exact state. This event-sourced architecture enables features like workflow versioning and time-travel debugging.
The tradeoff? More infrastructure to manage. You need to run a Temporal Server cluster (or use Temporal Cloud) separate from your application. But when you need it, nothing else comes close.

excerpts: LangGraph has a built-in persistence layer, implemented through checkpointers. When you compile a graph with a checkpointer, the checkpointer saves a `checkpoint` of the graph state at every super-step. Those checkpoints are saved to a `thread` , which can be accessed after graph execution. Because `threads` allow access to graph’s state after execution, several powerful capabilities including human-in-the-loop, memory, time travel, and fault-tolerance are all possible.
The state of a thread at a particular point in time is called a checkpoint. Checkpoint is a snapshot of the graph state saved at each super-step
Checkpoints are persisted and can be used to restore the state of a thread at a later time.

It’s also possible to play-back a prior graph execution. If we `invoke` a graph with a `thread_id` and a `checkpoint_id` , then we will *re-play* the previously executed steps *before* a checkpoint that corresponds to the `checkpoint_id` , and only execute the steps *after* the checkpoint.

Importantly, LangGraph knows whether a particular step has been executed previously. If it has, LangGraph simply *re-plays* that particular step in the graph and does not re-execute the step, but only for the steps *before* the provided `checkpoint_id` . All of the steps *after* `checkpoint_id` will be executed (i.e., a new fork), even if they have been executed previously.

Third, checkpointers allow for “time travel” , allowing users to replay prior graph executions to review and / or debug specific graph steps. In addition, checkpointers make it possible to fork the graph state at arbitrary checkpoints to explore alternative trajectories.

Lastly, checkpointing also provides fault-tolerance and error recovery: if one or more nodes fail at a given superstep, you can restart your graph from the last successful step. Additionally, when a graph node fails mid-execution at a given superstep, LangGraph stores pending checkpoint writes from any other nodes that completed successfully at that superstep, so that whenever we resume graph execution from that superstep we don’t re-run the successful nodes.

Additionally, when a graph node fails mid-execution at a given superstep, LangGraph stores pending checkpoint writes from any other nodes that completed successfully at that superstep, so that whenever we resume graph execution from that superstep we don’t re-run the successful nodes.

excerpts:
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > 6. Versioning and Evolution
Your agent’s logic will change. New version gets deployed while old workflows are still running. You need:
Version-aware execution (old workflows complete on old code)
Migration paths (how do in-flight workflows upgrade?)
Backward compatibility guarantees
As [Temporal’s team emphasizes](https://temporal.io/blog/building-an-agentic-system-thats-actually-production-ready) , production-ready means surviving not just crashes, but deployments and code evolution. Temporal provides explicit versioning mechanisms, while DBOS requires careful schema and code management.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Broader Ecosystem: Beyond Temporal and DBOS
While we’ve focused on Temporal and DBOS as exemplars, the durable execution landscape is rapidly evolving:
**Restate** — A newer event-driven durable execution platform with innovative state management approaches
**AWS Step Functions** — Managed workflows for AWS-native applications (though with vendor lock-in and less flexibility)
**Argo Workflows** — Kubernetes-native orchestration primarily for data and ML pipelines
**Dagster** — Data orchestration platform with durability primitives increasingly used for AI pipelines
**Airflow** — Traditional data workflow orchestrator, though designed for batch operations rather than real-time agentic systems
The trend is clear: **durable execution is becoming foundational infrastructure** for any stateful, autonomous system. Today, that’s primarily AI agents, but tomorrow it could be any long-running, reliable computation.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > The Heavyweight Approach: Orchestration at Scale
Now imagine you’re coordinating dozens of AI agents, each with complex branching logic, running for days, with sophisticated retry policies and human approval gates.
You need a different tool: a dedicated **orchestration engine** .
[Temporal](https://temporal.io/blog/build-resilient-agentic-ai-with-temporal) represents this heavyweight philosophy. It’s a standalone system designed specifically for managing complex, long-running workflows at scale.
What you get:
**Battle-tested reliability** — Used by companies running millions of workflows
**Rich workflow primitives** — Signals, queries, child workflows, activity retries
**Built-in observability** — Track every state transition in a polished UI
**Horizontal scalability** — Coordinate thousands of concurrent agents
**Strong consistency** — Guaranteed execution semantics through event sourcing
Temporal achieves durability through a different mechanism: every workflow operation gets logged as an event. When a workflow resumes after a failure, Temporal “replays” these events to reconstruct the exact state. This event-sourced architecture enables features like workflow versioning and time-travel debugging.
The tradeoff? More infrastructure to manage. You need to run a Temporal Server cluster (or use Temporal Cloud) separate from your application. But when you need it, nothing else comes close.
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > Centralized Orchestration: The Command Center
In a centralized model, one system acts as the “brain” — tracking all agent state, coordinating execution, and ensuring consistency.
[Temporal’s architecture](https://temporal.io/blog/a-mental-model-for-agentic-ai-applications) exemplifies this: a central Temporal Server manages workflow histories while worker nodes execute actual tasks. It’s like air traffic control for your AI agents.
**The wins:**
Single source of truth for what’s happening
Easy to debug (“show me the workflow history”)
Strong consistency guarantees
Unified monitoring and alerting
**The challenges:**
Orchestrator can become a bottleneck at extreme scale
More infrastructure to operate
Potential single point of coordination (though designed for high availability)
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > Decentralized Execution: Autonomous Agents
What if each agent could be self-sufficient, coordinating through shared state or events rather than a central controller?
That’s the decentralized model. [DBOS explores this](https://www.dbos.dev/blog/scaleable-decentralized-workflows) by enabling decentralized durability — agents coordinate through a shared database rather than an orchestrator, with checkpoints living close to execution.
**The wins:**
Scales horizontally without central bottleneck
Better fault isolation (one agent’s crash doesn’t impact others)
More flexible for geographically distributed systems
Lower coordination overhead
**The challenges:**
Harder to maintain global view of system state
Requires careful consistency management
Debugging becomes distributed tracing problem
Section Title: Durable AI Workflows: How to Build Resilient & Reliable AI Systems > 1. Durable State is Non-Negotiable
Your agent’s progress must survive crashes. Period. This means:
Checkpointing after every significant operation
Persisting intermediate results, not just final output
Using transactions where possible to ensure consistency
Temporal does this through event sourcing (every workflow operation is logged and can be replayed). DBOS does it through Postgres transactions (steps and checkpoints commit atomically). Choose your mechanism, but don’t skip this.

excerpts: Here’s what separates a basic router from a production-grade one. In February 2026, “which model” is no longer the only routing decision. Providers now expose inference-time compute controls, which means you need *two* decisions for every request.
evel 1** : Which model family/provider handles the task? **Level 2** : Within that model, what reasoning/compute level is warra

With our `TaskMeta` in hand, we now have enough signal to pick both a model *and* a compute level. This is where the two-level routing from the previous section becomes code. The router respects three budget tiers and maps each task type to the best model/compute combination for that tier.

Look at how the same task type gets different treatment depending on budget tier. A coding task on the “cheap” tier goes to MiniMax M2.5 with low reasoning. That same task on “quality” goes to Claude Opus 4.6 with high adaptive thinking. The router is making *both* decisions: which model and how hard to think.
Here’s the number that should make every engineering manager pay attention.

Research from the CLEAR paper on enterprise agentic evaluation found that optimizing solely for accuracy can yield systems 4.4x to 10.8x more expensive than cost-aware alternatives with comparable performance. Read that again. Comparable performance. Up to 10.8x the cost.

A router doesn’t need to be perfect to pay for itself when these deltas exist. It just needs to avoid consistently spending $25 output tokens on tasks that would be “good enough” at $1 to $3 output tokens.

The only thing getting more expensive is *not* routing.
